title: Chapter 14: The Lawyer Email
content: The letter arrives on a Tuesday, which feels wrong. Threats should come on Mondays, when you're already bracing, or Fridays, when you can at least drink about it. Tuesday is a nothing day. Tuesday is a day for returning library books and remembering you forgot to buy paper towels. Tuesday is not a day for legal action.
But here it is.

Maya finds it first, tucked between a grocery circular and a postcard from a dentist Chloe has never visited. The envelope is cream-colored, heavy stock, the kind of paper that costs more than the stamps on it. In the upper-left corner: WATKINS, PREECE & ASSOCIATES, LLP. A law firm. An actual law firm with an actual name that sounds like it was bred in a tank of older, more aggressive law firms.
"Chloe," Maya says, and the way she says it—quiet, careful, like she's holding something that might shatter—makes Chloe's ears flatten before she even sees the envelope.
Maya sets it on the kitchen table. They all look at it. The envelope just sits there, being cream-colored and legal.
"Open it," Elif says.
"You open it," Maya says.
"Absolutely not. I'm not legally involved in whatever this is."
"None of us are legally involved! It's addressed to Chloe!"

They both look at Dave, who is standing in the hallway with a towel around his shoulders because he was in the middle of washing his hair. "Don't look at me. I'm not even dressed for legal proceedings."
Chloe nudges the envelope with her nose. It smells like office. Like toner and recycled air and someone's lingering perfume from the mailroom. She pushes it toward Maya with a decisive nudge.
"Fine," Maya says. She slides her finger under the flap.

The letter is on letterhead. Of course it is. Letterhead that says WATKINS, PREECE & ASSOCIATES, LLP again, but now with an address in Midtown and a phone number and a fax number, because apparently this firm still faxes, which is either charming or threatening depending on your tolerance for anachronism. Below the letterhead, the text is justified, 11-point, probably Times New Roman, because evil always uses Times New Roman.
"Dear Ms. Kim," Maya reads aloud.
That's the last normal part.

The rest is a parade. A slow, methodical parade of words that Chloe used to type into emails herself, back when she was the one sending them from the other side. *Obligation.* *Unauthorized absence.* *Potential litigation.* Each one lands in the kitchen like a coin dropped on the floor—heavy, metallic, rolling somewhere you can't reach.

The letter claims three things. One: breach of employment contract, because apparently Chloe had an employment contract, which is news to Chloe, who mostly remembers signing a W-4 and a direct deposit form and something about the company handbook that she definitely didn't read. Two: failure to provide adequate notice, which is technically true, in the same way that a meteor technically fails to provide adequate notice before hitting the earth. Three: abandonment of active client files, a phrase so dramatic it sounds like she left orphans at a bus station instead of spreadsheets on a server.
The demands are numbered. They're numbered. Like a menu of consequences.
(a) Return to work within ten (10) business days.
Ten business days. Chloe's whole body goes still. Not capybara-still, which is her default, but the other kind. The kind where something inside locks and won't rotate.
(b) Complete exit interview and file transition documentation.

An exit interview. They want her to sit in a room and explain why she left, which—she can't sit in a room. She can't sit in *her own* room without disassociating. She certainly can't sit in *their* room, in *their* chair, across from *their* HR person who always had a tissue box on the desk like she knew, like she always knew people would need it.
(c) Face legal action to recover damages related to account disruption.
"Damages," Elif repeats. "Damages related to *account disruption*? What does that even mean? They're claiming you *damaged* them by quitting?"

Dave has emerged from the hallway, towel still on his shoulders, hair still wet and soapy. He's reading over Maya's shoulder now, and his face is doing the thing it does when he's thinking hard and doesn't want to show it—the thing where his jaw shifts slightly to the left and his eyes go flat and careful.
"This is insane," Elif says. "They can't sue you for quitting. People quit jobs. That's—it's a job, not a hostage situation."

"They can sue you for anything," Dave says. He reads the letter a second time, lips barely moving. "Whether it sticks is different. This is a demand letter, not a lawsuit. They're trying to scare you into coming back."
"It's working," Maya says quietly.
She's looking at Chloe.
Chloe is pacing.

She shouldn't be pacing. Capybaras don't pace. Capybaras are the physical manifestation of horizontal contentment, of the horizontal *idea* of contentment. But she's pacing—three steps toward the window, turn, three steps toward the refrigerator, turn, three steps back. Her claws click on the kitchen linoleum in a tight, repetitive rhythm. The distressed buzz is back, not low and manageable like it's been lately but loud, the volume turned up by the cream-colored envelope on the table. It's behind her eyes, in her jaw, in the space between her shoulders where the tension lives like a second spine.

She won't eat. Maya puts a slice of orange on the floor and Chloe walks over it. She won't bathe. Dave runs the tub and she doesn't even look toward the bathroom. The calm—the hard-won, weeks-in-the-making calm that she'd been building like a jigsaw puzzle, one piece at a time—is cracking. Not shattering. Cracking. Hairline fractures running through it, the kinds you don't notice until you press on them and the whole thing gives.
"Can we afford a lawyer?" Maya asks.
The question hangs in the kitchen like smoke.

Nobody answers, because the answer is no. They cannot. Maya is between freelance gigs. Elif's grad stipend covers rent and exactly one feeling of financial security per month, which she usually spends on the specific brand of yogurt she likes. Dave has savings, but Dave's savings are for Dave, which everyone knows and nobody resents and nobody is going to ask about. And Chloe—Chloe hasn't been paid in weeks. Her last paycheck went to rent. The one before that went to the vet bill when the capybara body decided to have opinions about its own digestive system.

"We need to tell someone," Dave says. He's dry now, hair sticking up in weird directions, no longer the person who was in the middle of anything. "Legal someone. Even a law student. Even a guy who watched one season of Suits."
"I have a cousin," Maya says. "She's a lawyer."
"Employment law?"
"Tenant law."
"That's not—"
"She's a lawyer, Dave. She passed the bar. She's admitted to practice in the state of New York. I will take tenant law over no law right now."

Maya calls from the hallway. The rest of them stay in the kitchen, watching Chloe pace, watching the orange slice sit on the floor untouched, watching the cream-colored envelope be cream-colored and envelope-shaped on the table.
Through the wall, they can hear Maya's half of the conversation. Fragmentary. "Yeah, no, she's a capybara—no, like, an actual—yes, I'm serious—no, I'm not high—look, can I just read you the letter?"
She comes back ten minutes later with notes on her phone.
"She said: don't respond. Don't call them, don't email them, don't send a carrier pigeon. Any response confirms receipt and engagement. Right now, they don't even know if this letter reached you."
"I mean," Elif says, "it reached us. We opened it. It's right there."
"She said *they* don't know that. There's no tracking. No certified mail. It's a regular envelope. For all they know, it fell behind a mailbox."
Dave nods slowly. "That's actually smart."

"She also said: document everything. Every email they sent before this, every voicemail, every weird Slack message from a manager asking if you're 'okay' while clearly fishing for information about when you're coming back. Forward it all to a personal account if you still have access."
"Chloe doesn't have access," Maya says. "They deactivated her credentials the day after she stopped showing up."
"Of course they did," Dave mutters.
"And—" Maya hesitates. "She said to get Chloe's resignation in writing. Even retroactively."
Elif blinks. "Retroactive resignation?"

"If there's no paper trail of Chloe quitting, then technically she just… disappeared. Which looks worse. A formal resignation letter, even dated back, creates a narrative. She resigned. She didn't abandon. There's a difference."

Chloe has stopped pacing. She's standing in the corner of the kitchen, pressed against the wall where the radiator used to hiss before the super fixed it. Her breathing is fast and shallow. The buzz is so loud she can't hear the apartment around her—can't hear Elif saying her name, can't hear Dave pulling out a chair, can't hear anything except the white-noise roar of *potential litigation* and *account disruption* and *business days*.
She can't write.

The realization arrives not as a thought but as a physical sensation—a dropping, a hollowing, the way a stomach drops on a roller coaster but without the fun part before it. She can't write. She can barely text. Her hooves—her paws—whatever they are now are built for standing on riverbanks and eating grass, not for gripping pens or typing on keyboards. She couldn't write this letter if she wanted to. She cannot produce the document that might protect her from the cream-colored envelope on the table.
"I'll type it," Maya says.

She's already at her laptop. She doesn't ask Chloe what to say. She knows. She's been living in the same apartment, watching the same person spiral, for weeks. She knows what Chloe would say if Chloe could say it.

The letter is short. Professional. Boring, even. It says: I, Chloe Kim, am resigning from my position effective [date three weeks ago]. I have appreciated the opportunities etc. I wish the team etc. Please direct any correspondence to my home address. It sounds like every other resignation letter ever written, which is the point.
Maya prints it. The printer makes its usual protest—grinding, wheezing, a sound like a cat being indignant about a closed door—but eventually produces a single sheet of paper.
She signs it. Her own name, then, below it, in smaller letters: *on behalf of Chloe Kim, per her verbal authorization.*

It's flimsy. They all know it's flimsy. It's a roommate typing a letter for a woman who is currently a large rodent and signing it with a proxy clause that would make any real lawyer wince. It's a bandage on a wound that might need stitches. It's something.

Chloe reads it—or, more accurately, looks at it for a long time, her dark eyes moving across the lines, her nose twitching once at the smell of fresh printer ink. Then she nods. A single, slow dip of her massive head.
Maya puts the letter in a folder. The folder goes in a drawer. The drawer closes.
The cream-colored envelope is still on the table. Nobody moves it. It sits there, being legal and threatening, for the rest of the day.

That night, Chloe doesn't sleep. She lies on her bed—which is just a mattress on the floor now, because the frame couldn't hold her—and stares at the ceiling and thinks about ten business days and the word *abandonment* and how a letter can weigh less than an orange slice and still feel like it's pressing on her chest.
Somewhere around 3 AM, she gets up and eats the orange slice from the kitchen floor. It's warm and a little fuzzy from sitting out all day. She eats it anyway.
🔔 *Satisfaction: Small* — The cousin's advice, the roommates trying. But anxiety is winning.
